mappings.rationaleAI: St Paul's missionary journeys, theology of justification by faith, and role in opening Christianity to Gentiles directly address the origins of the primitive church (A.1.a), post-apostolic developments (A.1.b), and Christology (F.1.c).
Constantine's Edict of Milan, founding of Constantinople, and Council of Nicaea directly address the establishment of Christianity as state religion (A.2.a) and formal statements of beliefs (E.4).

The Nicene Creed covers the Arian controversy, the Councils of Nicaea and Constantinople, and the Filioque dispute — directly relevant to doctrinal controversies (A.2.b), creeds (E.4), and the Trinity (F.1.f).
Arianism and the Council of Nicaea's formulation of the Nicene Creed directly address doctrinal controversies including reaction to Arius's teachings (A.2.b).
The Trinity doctrine from Church Fathers through Nicaea and Constantinople directly addresses the doctrine of the Holy Trinity (F.1.f) and doctrinal controversies (A.2.b).
The Pelagian Controversy between Pelagius and Augustine over free will and grace directly addresses doctrinal controversies (A.2.b) and doctrines concerning mankind (F.4).
The Fall and original sin from Genesis through Augustine and Reformation disputes directly addresses doctrines concerning mankind (F.4) and biblical exegesis (E.2).
Byzantium covers the growing division between Eastern and Western Christianity (A.3), the church of imperial Byzantium (B.1), and Eastern Orthodoxy's general characteristics (G.1).
The Great Schism of 1054 directly addresses the Photian schism and East-West division (A.3.d) and the church of imperial Byzantium (B.1).
The Jesuits as Counter-Reformation order with global missionary work directly addresses the Catholic Reformation (C.2.c) and monasticism/the saintly life (F.6.c).
Wyclif's challenge to Catholic orthodoxy and advocacy for vernacular Bible translation directly addresses the background of the Protestant Reformation (D.1.a) and biblical exegesis (E.2).
Catharism as a medieval dualist heresy challenging papal authority directly addresses the development of the papacy and religious movements within medieval Latin Christianity (C.1.a).
The Book of Common Prayer under Cranmer during the English Reformation directly addresses the English Reformation (D.1.d) and the role of liturgy (F.6.b).
Purgatory covers eschatological doctrine (F.1.d) and its Protestant rejection during the Reformation (C.1.b/D.1).
George Fox and the Quakers represent a radical reform movement (D.1.f) and the Society of Friends as a Free church variation (G.3.d.iv).
Deism as the Enlightenment challenge to revealed religion directly addresses the challenge of rationalism, Deism, and the Enlightenment (D.2.c).
John Wesley and Methodism directly addresses developments in English Protestantism (D.2.d) and Methodists as a Free church (G.3.d.iv).
Religious fundamentalism's origins in American Protestant movements defending biblical inerrancy directly addresses fundamentalist and evangelical groups (G.3.e.vii).
The Rapture as dispensationalist eschatology directly addresses eschatological doctrines (F.1.d) and millenarian churches (G.3.e.iii).
Biblical typology as an interpretive method directly addresses biblical exegesis and hermeneutics (E.2).
Angels in Christianity including hierarchies and the fall of Lucifer directly address doctrines concerning intermediary beings (F.2).
The spread of religious doubt covers the Enlightenment challenge to Christianity and Victorian crisis of faith — relevant to the challenge of rationalism (D.2.c) and the modern age (D.3).
Comenius as bishop of the Moravian Brethren during the Thirty Years' War represents radical Protestant reform movements (D.1.f).
The Tudor State's break with Rome, dissolution of the monasteries, and Elizabethan settlement directly address the English Reformation (D.1.d).
The Divine Right of Kings covers the theological doctrine of monarchical authority from God and church-state relations — relevant to church-state relations during the wars of religion (D.1.h).
